coverage_note: Complete verification. Compared DB text (id 18147) character-by-character against authoritative PDF source using pdftotext -layout extraction. flags.description: Citations are systematically split across multiple lines with standalone punctuation marks. Line 119 contains '¶ 9', line 120 contains standalone ',', line 121 contains '898 N.W.2d 700'. These should be joined as '2017 ND 175, ¶ 9, 898 N.W.2d 700' on one or two properly reflowed lines, as shown in the PDF source (pdftotext output lines 111-112). This pattern occurs 14+ times throughout the opinion (at least 30+ related instances of standalone punctuation on their own lines).

Citation for 'Nationwide Mut. Ins. Cos. v. Lagodinski, 2004 ND 147, ¶' is fragmented across lines 123-128 in the DB. The '7,' on line 128 is the continuation of what should be a page number marker ('2' on line 127) followed by text resumption. In the source PDF (pdftotext), this appears as: '2004 ND 147, ¶' on line 112, followed by form-feed and page header, then '7, 683 N.W.2d 903' on line 116. The DB is missing the form-feed character (0x0C) that separates pages, causing text to run together awkwardly.

flags.description: DB contains metadata header (lines 1-6) not present in the authoritative PDF source: '# Queen v. Martel', 'North Dakota Supreme Court', '986 N.W.2d 98; 2023 ND 32', 'Decided 2023-02-23'. These appear to be processing/ingestion artifacts added during database entry. Per rule 2 (FIDELITY to printed source), these metadata lines should be removed if the goal is to match the authoritative PDF. However, they do not corrupt the opinion text itself and may be intentional database metadata.

PDF has statute reference 'N.D.C.C. § 14-' split across line break to '09-06.2(1)(j)' on the next line (PDF lines 51-52), while DB has it consolidated as '§ 14-09-06.2(1)(j)' on line 58. This is NOT a textual error—the citation itself is identical in both; it is merely a layout difference from the pdftotext extraction reflecting the PDF's line-break formatting. No fix needed.

Quote character policy: DB uses straight ASCII quotes (U+0027) throughout (e.g., 'Martel's' and 'court's' on lines 1886 and 2329 respectively), while the PDF source contains curly/typographic right single quotes (U+2019). Per rule 9, this difference is INTENTIONAL (DB policy uses straight quotes) and should NOT be flagged or proposed for change.

flags.description: PDF file (2023ND19.pdf) contains character encoding corruption in paragraphs 18-21 where the pilcrow symbol (¶) has been corrupted to 'if' in the rendered text (e.g., '[if18]' instead of '[¶18]'), and multiple words have been split with internal spaces (e.g., 'commun ity' for 'community', 'guardian ship' for 'guardianship', 'caretake r' for 'caretaker', 'erroneou s' for 'erroneous', 'discretio n' for 'discretion', 'appointin g' for 'appointing'). The DB text correctly preserves the pilcrow markers and proper word forms. This indicates a font rendering or PDF generation issue in the source file itself, but the DB ingestion process successfully recovered the correct text.

PDF contains a substitution error in statute citation where 'f' appears as '£' (pound sterling symbol) in paragraphs 19-20. The DB correctly preserves the 'f' character. This suggests a character encoding/font substitution problem in the PDF file.

PDF shows '[r' and ',r' (comma + r) instead of correct pilcrow symbols in paragraph 19 and 20 when citing to other paragraphs. The DB correctly shows '¶ 16', '¶ 17', etc. This is consistent with the pilcrow encoding corruption affecting the entire PDF.

The PDF shows 'annuities income' as a separate item from 'workers' compensation benefits', but the DB has corrupted this into a single malformed phrase. The change preserves all three terms correctly while maintaining exact byte count.

The PDF clearly shows the citation on a single line without the erroneous newlines that appear in the DB. The PDF reads: 'Storing v. National Surety Co., 56 ND 14, 215 N.W.\n875 (1927)' while the DB has artificially split the citation across multiple lines with '
56 ND 14
,
215 N.W.
875
' breaking up the reference sequence. This is clearly an ingestion/OCR artifact where line breaks from the PDF layout were incorrectly preserved as hard line breaks in prose. The fix restores the proper continuous citation format.

flags.description: The PDF source contains form-feed (page break) characters (6 total) followed by running headers repeating the case caption. These are standard PDF layout artifacts handled by separate deterministic pipeline. DB correctly omits these. Not a defect.

coverage_note: Comprehensive analysis: extracted database text (423 lines) and authoritative PDF source (354 lines, pdftotext -layout). Performed character-by-character comparison with whitespace normalization, paragraph-level verification (all 19 paragraphs present), citation format checking, and OCR error detection. Only one substantive error found. No OCR character substitutions, no missing text, no digit errors detected.

proposals.old_exact: 1Rule 52(4), N.D. Sup. Ct. Admin. R., was subsequently amended. See Joint Procedure Committee
proposals.new_exact: 1 Rule 52(4), N.D. Sup. Ct. Admin. R., was subsequently amended. See Joint Procedure Committee
proposals.evidence: The PDF source shows '1 Rule 52(4)' with a clear space between the footnote marker '1' and the word 'Rule'. The DB has lost this space, creating '1Rule' which is an OCR/ingestion transcription error. This is a footnote beginning text.
proposals.note: Footnote number marker missing space before 'Rule'. Single occurrence confirmed by byte-exact search.

flags.description: The citation 'v. Oien, 2005 ND 205, ¶ 8, 706 N.W.2d 81.' is split across multiple lines in the DB (lines 137-140) but appears on a single line in the PDF (line 131). Additionally, there are spaces inserted before commas and periods: '2005 ND 205, ¶ 8' becomes '2005 ND 205, ¶ 8' + newline + ',' instead of being on one line. Similarly 'State v. Chacano, 2012 ND 113, ¶ 10, 817 N.W.2d 369.' is split into lines 142-147.

proposals.evidence: The PDF image shows 'trial exhibits 10-14¹' where the superscript 1 is a footnote marker, not a digit. The pdftotext tool incorrectly rendered the superscript footnote marker as a digit, creating '10-141'. This is confirmed by: (1) ¶15 correctly refers to 'trial exhibits 10-14' (same exhibits); (2) Footnote 1 states 'exhibits 10-14 are the documents'; (3) The opinion consistently uses '10-14' elsewhere. The single instance of '10-141' in ¶13 is an OCR artifact from superscript-to-digit confusion.
proposals.note: This is a classic OCR error where pdftotext converted a superscript footnote number to a digit. The exhibits are correctly identified as 10-14 throughout the opinion.

flags.description: Structural difference: DB includes parsed header information ('# State v. Bowen', 'North Dakota Supreme Court', '985 N.W.2d 636; 2023 ND 25', 'Decided 2023-02-16') followed by the standard FILED/CLERK text. The PDF uses right-aligned formatting with the FILED/CLERK block at top and case caption centered below. This is a structural/formatting artifact of database ingestion, not a content error. The substantive opinion text is identical.

coverage_note: Full opinion reviewed (18,131 characters, 19 paragraphs). Structural comparison performed on header differences (formatting artifact). Substantive opinion content compared character-by-character after normalization. All citations, dates, paragraph numbering, and cross-references verified as accurate. One OCR error identified and confirmed against authoritative PDF source."

proposals.old_exact: 20230014 FILED FEBRUARY 9, 2023 CLERK OF THE SUPREME COURT STATE OF NORTH DAKOTAreport
proposals.source: PDF pdftotext -layout output, paragraph 4 (lines 39-45 in source). The running header/footer docket number, filing date, and court name were smushed onto the end of '[¶4] No comments or petitions were received to relocate or abolish this judgeship. On January 31, 2023, the South Central Judicial District filed a' during OCR ingestion. The source shows these elements separated by page break (\x0c, line 42) and line breaks; the word 'report' should follow directly.
proposals.note: This is a page furniture artifact (header/footer text injected mid-sentence). The page break and running header are stripped in the clean text; only the erroneous concatenation of header text to 'report' needs fixing.
